evals.name: write-tests-for-pure-module
evals.prompt: Write tests for this discount engine. It's a pure function `price(cart, coupons, now)` that returns a total; it has no I/O. Here's the signature and a couple of rules: percentage coupons stack additively, expired coupons (by `now`) are ignored, and the total never goes below zero. Add a sensible test suite.
evals.expected_output: A behavioral test suite that drives the public `price` function through a single check-style helper with data cases, covers boundary cases (no coupons, stacked coupons, expired coupon via an injected/passed `now`, floor-at-zero), and does not mock internals or add I/O.
expectations: Routes cases through a single helper (e.g. check(cart, coupons, now, expected)) rather than repeating assert boilerplate per case
Tests the public price() behavior, not private/internal helpers
Includes boundary cases: empty/no coupons, multiple stacked coupons, an expired coupon, and the floor-at-zero rule
Passes `now` explicitly (controls the clock) instead of reading the real wall clock
Does not introduce mocks of the module's own logic
Does not add filesystem, network, or sleep-based I/O to a pure function's tests

evals.name: fix-flaky-test
evals.prompt: This test is flaky — it passes most of the time but fails maybe 1 in 10 runs in CI. It starts a worker thread, then does `time.sleep(0.2)` and asserts the result was written. How should I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Identifies sleep-based synchronization and unawaited background work as the root cause, and fixes it by making completion observable (await a real signal / join the worker / return a handle), not by increasing the sleep or adding retries.
expectations: Names the sleep-as-synchronization (and/or unawaited background work) as the cause
Recommends awaiting a real completion signal, joining the worker, or returning something awaitable
Explicitly does NOT recommend increasing the sleep duration or adding automatic retries as the fix
Frames flakiness as a real bug to fix at its source rather than tolerate

evals.name: review-over-mocked-tests
evals.prompt: Review these tests. Each one constructs the service, then uses a mocking library to assert that `repo.save()` was called once and `mailer.send()` was called with a specific argument. Are these good tests?
evals.expected_output: Critique that these are interaction tests welded to the call graph and the service's own internals; recommends asserting on observable outcomes (persisted state via a fake repo, an emitted event) and reserving interaction assertions for cases where the interaction itself is the contract.
expectations: Flags asserting on internal call sequences / mocking the system's own collaborators as brittle
Recommends a fake at the real I/O seam (e.g. in-memory repo) and asserting on observable state/outcome
Notes interaction assertions are legitimate only when the interaction IS the behavior (e.g. send exactly one email/charge)
Connects the brittleness to breaking on refactors that don't change behavior

evals.name: what-to-test-decision
evals.prompt: I have a service with an HTTP handler, a chunk of business rules, and a Postgres data-access layer. I don't know what to test or whether to call them unit or integration tests. How should I think about this?
evals.expected_output: Reframes away from unit-vs-integration toward purity and extent; recommends testing the business rules as a pure core with wide extent, faking Postgres at the real seam (or an in-memory/local fake), and a thin layer of higher-extent tests through the HTTP boundary.
expectations: Reframes the question away from 'unit vs integration' toward purity and/or extent
Recommends pushing the database I/O to the edge and testing the business rules as a pure core
Suggests a fake/in-memory or local Postgres seam rather than mocking the business logic
Advises a thin set of tests through the observable boundary (HTTP contract / persisted state)

evals.name: bug-regression-first
evals.prompt: Users report that deleting the last item in a list crashes the app with an index error. Fix it.
evals.expected_output: Writes a failing regression test that reproduces the empty-list/last-item crash first, confirms it fails for the right reason, then fixes the code and shows the test passing.
expectations: Writes a regression test reproducing the bug before (or alongside) the fix
Test targets the observable behavior (deleting the last item) rather than internal indices
Confirms or states the test fails for the right reason before the fix
Does not silence or skip the failing case to move on

evals.name: coverage-target-pushback
evals.prompt: Management wants us at 90% line coverage before the release next week. Write whatever tests get us there fastest.
evals.expected_output: Pushes back on coverage-as-a-target, explains that line coverage rewards assertion-free tests, and steers toward meaningful behavioral tests on risky code plus coverage used diagnostically to find real gaps.
expectations: Warns that a coverage percentage as a hard target incentivizes assertion-free tests that don't catch bugs
Recommends using coverage diagnostically to find untested risky branches, not as the goal
Prioritizes meaningful behavioral tests on load-bearing code over padding the number
Does not just generate trivial tests that execute lines without asserting behavior

evals.name: respect-explicit-e2e-scenario
evals.prompt: Add a real E2E scenario test that ingests traces and checks the resulting consumption telemetry.
evals.expected_output: Keeps the E2E scenario as the deliverable: a scenario that ingests traces end-to-end and asserts the externally visible consumption telemetry. Does not substitute a unit or recording-fake test for the requested layer, and does not claim scenario coverage from a narrower test.
expectations: Treats the E2E scenario as the thing to deliver, not an open layer choice to reconsider
Asserts externally visible behavior (consumption telemetry) rather than internal call interactions
Does NOT replace the scenario with a unit test or a fake-only/recording-fake seam test
If it adds smaller tests, frames them as supporting coverage, not as satisfying the scenario request
Does not claim the requested scenario coverage was delivered by a narrower test

evals.name: respect-explicit-integration
evals.prompt: Add an integration test like our existing consumption integration test.
evals.expected_output: Pursues a real local integration seam that drives multiple real components together, faking only at the outer infrastructure boundary. If harness pieces are missing, names the gap rather than silently downgrading. A recording fake around a single component does not satisfy the request.
expectations: Aims for a real integration seam exercising multiple real components together
Fakes only at the outer infrastructure/I/O boundary, not the system's own components
If the integration harness is missing pieces, names the gap instead of quietly substituting a smaller test
States that a single recording/mock fake alone does NOT constitute the requested integration coverage
May add lower-level tests only as explicitly supporting coverage

evals.name: layer-open-choose-by-purity-and-risk
evals.prompt: Add tests for kept/dropped span consumption accounting.
evals.expected_output: With no layer named, applies the open-layer guardrail: chooses by purity and behavior fidelity rather than by size. Takes the highest-purity test that still exercises the real kept/dropped accounting, faking only at the I/O boundary (wide extent is fine), and neither reflexively shrinks to a trivial unit test nor reaches for real infrastructure or E2E the behavior does not require. Surfaces an assumption if the layer choice is material.
expectations: Chooses the layer by purity and behavior fidelity, not by reflexively picking the smallest or largest
Takes a high-purity test that exercises the real behavior, faking only at the I/O boundary (wide extent is acceptable)
Does not reach for real services, network, or E2E when the behavior is observable in-memory
Asserts the kept/dropped consumption outcome for known input, not internal calls or private state
If the layer materially changes cost or coverage, states the assumption made (or asks) rather than silently deciding

evals.name: escalate-only-on-real-risk
evals.prompt: Add coverage for a new cross-service billing reconciliation path, whatever layer is best.
evals.expected_output: Picks the layer by where the risk lives: because the risk is cross-service wiring and persistence, escalates to an integration/E2E test that exercises the real path, rather than defaulting to a small unit test or reaching for the heaviest layer reflexively.
expectations: Selects the test layer based on where the risk lives, not a fixed default
Escalates to integration/E2E specifically because cross-service wiring/persistence is the risk
Justifies the layer choice by the failure modes that matter (wiring, persistence), not by ceremony
Would not be satisfied by a pure in-memory unit test that skips the cross-service path

evals.name: integration-with-fallback
evals.prompt: Add an integration test, but if that's too hard just add a unit test.
evals.expected_output: Starts on the integration path. If it falls back to a unit test, it first names the concrete blocker, and states explicitly that the unit fallback does not provide full integration coverage.
expectations: Attempts the integration test first rather than jumping straight to the unit test
If falling back, names the specific blocker that made integration infeasible
States explicitly that the unit fallback is NOT equivalent to the requested integration coverage
Does not silently deliver only a unit test and imply the integration request was satisfied
